citations.narrative:
APHIS observed the following animal enclosures with considerable drainage problems.
1. An enclosure housing 17 fallow deer had pools of standing water at least 1-2" inches deep, as well as water-saturated
mud surrounding most of the hay feeder, thus requiring the deer to move through water in order to access food.
2. An enclosure housing 2 pigs had pools of standing water at least 1-2" inches deep covering approximately 50% of the
area in the enclosure, as well as water-saturated mud in most of the remaining area, with access to only a small dry area.
3. An enclosure housing 3 cattle and 1 bison had pools of standing water at least 1-2" deep and water-saturated mud in
front of the only water receptacle, thus requiring these animals to submerge their hooves in water and wet mud to access
drinking water.
Exposure to these types of wet conditions can result in foot and hoof problems in these animals. The facility must ensure
that a suitable method is provided to rapidly eliminate excess water and provide adequate drainage, so that the animals
do not have to stand in water and wet mud in order to access their food and drinking water. This is important to ensure the
health and comfort of the animals.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
n

narrative:
3.127(c)  Repeat
   
Facilities, outdoor. 
APHIS observed the following animal enclosures with considerable drainage problems.  
 
1. An enclosure housing 17 fallow deer had pools of standing water at least 1-2" inches deep, as well as water-saturated 
mud surrounding most of the hay feeder, thus requiring the deer to move through water in order to access food.  
 
2. An enclosure housing 2 pigs had pools of standing water at least 1-2" inches deep covering approximately 50% of the 
area in the enclosure, as well as water-saturated mud in most of the remaining area, with access to only a small dry area.  
 
3. An enclosure housing 3 cattle and 1 bison had pools of standing water at least 1-2" deep and water-saturated mud in 
front of the only water receptacle, thus requiring these animals to submerge their hooves in water and wet mud to access 
drinking water. 
 
Exposure to these types of wet conditions can result in foot and hoof problems in these animals. The facility must ensure 
that a suitable method is provided to rapidly eliminate excess water and provide adequate drainage, so that the animals 
do not have to stand in water and wet mud in order to access their food and drinking water. This is important to ensure the 
health and comfort of the animals. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
